title: Evaluation Schemes, Criteria, and Techniques
narrationScript: Finding an indication is the easy half of visual testing. What you do next, in what order, and against what rulebook, is what separates a certified examiner from a tourist with a flashlight. This lesson is about the evaluation machinery. The vocabulary follows the standard terminology used across NDT, the same scheme reflected in ASTM's terminology standard. Here is the chain, and the order is not negotiable. First comes the indication, anything you observe that requires interpretation. Then comes interpretation, where you decide what produced the indication. A false indication comes from something that is not a real condition of the part at all, glare, smeared paint, a polishing sheen. A nonrelevant indication is real but comes from geometry or a condition the criteria do not address, a machining groove, the shadow of a weld ripple. A relevant indication comes from an actual discontinuity, and only relevant indications move forward. Next comes evaluation, where you compare the relevant indication against the acceptance criteria that govern the job. And only then comes disposition. If the discontinuity violates the criteria, it is rejectable, and now, finally, it has earned the word defect. A discontinuity is not a defect until evaluation says so. Keep those words straight, because overcalling scraps good hardware and undercalling ships bad hardware, and both mistakes carry your signature. Now, the criteria themselves come in three styles, and most real codes mix them. The first style is the verbal description. No cracks. No incomplete fusion. Free of crater pits. These are attribute rules, binary, pass or fail. They are fast, but the edges get fuzzy. What does visible mean. So a good procedure pins down the lighting, the viewing distance, and the magnification that make the call reproducible. The second style is the comparison standard or catalog. Here you grade the part against a physical replica or a set of reference photographs. Think cast surface comparator plates, surface roughness specimens, weld workmanship sample boards. You hold the reference next to the part, match the lighting and the viewing angle, and assign the nearest grade. Then you record which reference set you used and the grade you gave. The third style is the size based criterion. Undercut no deeper than a stated limit. Porosity no larger than a stated diameter. Reinforcement within a height range. Pit depth against remaining wall. Size based criteria force you to measure, and your tool has to resolve finer than the limit you are checking. Checking a limit of one millimeter with a rule graduated in whole millimeters is guessing, not evaluating. And then there are mixtures, which is what you will actually meet in the field. A single acceptance paragraph may tell you no cracks at all, undercut within a depth limit, and surface texture per a sample board, all at once. Read the whole paragraph before you start and build a checklist so every clause gets evaluated against every relevant indication. The most common field error with mixed criteria is anchoring on the clause that is easiest to check and forgetting the rest. Techniques follow the criteria. Visual tactile recognition uses eyes and fingertips together. A fingernail dragged across a weld toe finds sharp undercut your eye misses, and a fingertip reads mismatch steps in poor access. Grading by comparison means disciplined side by side viewing with the reference standard. Measurement means tools. Steel rules and calipers. Fillet weld gauges for leg and throat. Bridge cam gauges for reinforcement height, undercut, and preparation angles. Dedicated undercut gauges. Pit gauges. High low gauges for fit up mismatch. Optical comparators and measuring magnifiers for fine work. And calibrated measuring functions on video borescopes for remote work, remembering that remote measurement accuracy depends on tip to target calibration. Match the technique to what the criterion asks. An attribute clause needs recognition. A grade clause needs comparison. A number clause needs a calibrated measurement. One closing rule. The criteria that count are the ones invoked by the contract, the specified document, edition, and paragraph. Not the newest book on the shelf, and not your memory of a different job. Write the criteria reference on the report. In the practice scenario you will walk a weld toe indication through the whole chain, interpretation, criteria style, technique choice, and final disposition. Take the steps in order, every time, and the right call takes care of itself.

sections.heading: The Evaluation Chain
sections.body:
Every visual examination runs the same pipeline, using the vocabulary standardized in documents like ASTM E1316:

- **Indication** — anything you observe that requires interpretation. Neutral word; no judgment yet.
- **Interpretation** — deciding what produced the indication. Three outcomes: **false** — produced by something other than an actual condition of the part, such as glare, smeared paint, or polishing sheen; **nonrelevant** — real, but produced by geometry or a condition the criteria do not address, such as a machining groove or the shadow line of a weld ripple; or **relevant** — produced by an actual discontinuity.
- **Evaluation** — comparing a relevant indication against the governing acceptance criteria.
- **Disposition** — accept, reject, or rework. A discontinuity that violates the criteria is rejectable, and only then does it earn the name **defect**.

The order is not negotiable. Evaluating before interpreting wastes effort on glare; interpreting without evaluating leaves rejectable conditions undispositioned. The economics cut both ways: overcalling — treating every indication as a defect — scraps good hardware and stops production for nothing, while undercalling ships hardware that fails in service. Both failures carry your signature, which is why the scheme exists: it forces a defensible, repeatable decision at every step instead of one leap of judgment.

sections.caption: The evaluation pipeline: an indication is interpreted as false, nonrelevant, or relevant, and only relevant indications are evaluated against acceptance criteria — a discontinuity becomes a defect only when it is rejectable.

sections.heading: Criteria Style One: Verbal Descriptions
sections.body:
The simplest criteria are verbal attribute statements: **no cracks**; **no incomplete fusion**; **free of crater pits**; **no visible porosity**. There is no number to measure — the condition is either present or it is not, and presence means rejection.

Strengths: fast, conceptually unambiguous, and the right tool for conditions that are never acceptable at any size, like cracks. Weaknesses: the edges get fuzzy in practice. What counts as **visible**? At what distance, under what lighting, with what magnification? A disciplined procedure closes those gaps by specifying examination conditions — minimum illuminance, maximum eye-to-surface distance, viewing angle, and whether magnification is used — so two examiners reach the same call on the same weld.

Watch the definitions, too. Many documents define a **linear indication** as one with length at least three times its width, and some apply different rules to linear versus rounded indications. The definition in the governing document — not a remembered one — controls your classification.

sections.heading: Criteria Style Two: Comparison Standards and Catalogs
sections.body:
The second criteria style replaces words with a physical or photographic reference: grade the part by comparing it with a standard.

- **Replica-type standards** — cast or electroformed comparator plates for casting surfaces, surface-roughness comparator specimens, and shop-built workmanship samples for weld appearance. Replicas respond to light the way the real surface does, and you can run a fingertip across them — a genuine advantage for texture judgments.
- **Photographic catalogs** — graded photo series of surface conditions. Cheap, portable, and easy to issue to every examiner, though lighting in a photo never exactly matches lighting on your part.

The technique is disciplined side-by-side viewing: hold the reference next to the part, match lighting and viewing angle, and assign the nearest grade. Then record three things — the identity of the reference set, including its serial if controlled, the grade assigned, and the criteria clause that invoked it. Comparison references are controlled items: they must be the set the contract names, in usable condition, and traceable in your report.

sections.heading: Criteria Style Three: Size-Based — and Real-World Mixtures
sections.body:
The third style is numeric. The criterion states a measurable limit, and your job is to produce a number:

- Maximum single pore diameter; cumulative porosity length per unit of weld length.
- Undercut depth limits — structural codes commonly cap undercut at around one thirty-second of an inch for many member types.
- Weld reinforcement height ranges; fillet leg and throat sizes.
- Pit depth against minimum remaining wall.

Size-based criteria force measurement, and the tool must resolve meaningfully finer than the limit — checking a 0.8 mm undercut allowance with a rule graduated in whole millimeters is not evaluation, it is guessing.

In the field you will rarely meet one style alone. Real acceptance paragraphs are **mixtures**: no cracks regardless of size — verbal; undercut not to exceed a stated depth — size-based; surface texture at least equal to sample board number three — comparison. Treat a mixed paragraph as a checklist: list every clause, decide which technique each clause demands, and evaluate every relevant indication against every applicable clause. The most common field error with mixed criteria is anchoring on the clause that is easiest to check — the porosity gets measured while the texture clause never gets graded.

sections.title: The Criteria Are Contractual
sections.body: Acceptance criteria come from the code edition and project specification invoked by the contract — not from memory, not from a different job, and not from the newest edition on the shelf. Limits change between editions. Record the document, edition, and paragraph you evaluated against on the report; an evaluation without its criteria reference cannot be audited or repeated.

sections.heading: Evaluation Techniques: Tactile, Grading, Measurement
sections.body:
CP-105 lists three evaluation techniques, and they map one-to-one onto the criteria styles:

- **Visual-tactile recognition.** Eyes plus fingertips. A fingernail dragged across a weld toe catches sharp undercut that flat lighting hides; a fingertip reads mismatch and step changes smaller than the eye resolves in poor access. Tactile checks supplement — never replace — the lighting and viewing-geometry requirements of the procedure, and they happen only where it is safe to touch.
- **Grading by comparison with a standard.** The side-by-side discipline from the previous section: same lighting, same angle, nearest grade, reference identity recorded.
- **Measurement.** The toolbox: steel rule and calipers for lengths; fillet weld gauges for leg and throat; bridge-cam (cambridge) gauges for reinforcement height, undercut, and preparation angles; dedicated undercut gauges such as the V-WAC; pit gauges for pit depth; Hi-Lo gauges for fit-up mismatch; measuring magnifiers and optical comparators with reticles for fine surface dimensions; and calibrated measurement modes on video borescopes for remote work — remembering that remote measurement accuracy hangs on tip-to-target distance calibration.

Selection logic: an attribute clause needs recognition, a grading clause needs a comparison standard, a numeric clause needs a calibrated measurement. If you cannot name the clause a tool serves, either the tool is wrong or the criteria have not been read carefully enough.

sections.caption: Matching criteria styles to techniques and tools.
rows: No cracks; free of crater pits
Surface at least equal to comparator Grade B
Comparator plates, photo catalogs, workmanship samples
Undercut depth not to exceed 1/32 in.
V-WAC, bridge-cam gauge, pit gauge, measuring magnifier
No cracks; porosity within 2 mm; texture per sample board
Full kit plus a clause checklist

figures.title: From Indication to Disposition: The Evaluation Scheme

interactive.title: Walk the Chain: A Weld-Toe Indication
interactive.intro: You are the VT Level II on a structural fabrication job. The governing acceptance criteria for fillet welds read: no cracks or crater cracks; undercut depth not to exceed 1 mm; single pore diameter not to exceed 2 mm; weld surface texture at least equal to the workmanship sample board. Work the next find in the proper order.

steps.prompt: At a fillet weld toe you spot a dark linear mark about 4 mm long. What is your first move?
options: Write it up immediately as a rejectable defect — linear marks at weld toes are cracks until proven otherwise
Clean the area, set up procedure lighting, and interpret whether the indication is false, nonrelevant, or relevant
Have the welder lightly grind the area so the mark is removed before anyone else sees it
steps.feedback: Interpretation comes before evaluation — a dark mark could be smeared paint or a wire-brush artifact, and calling it a defect now skips two steps of the chain. Grinding it out first is worse: it destroys the evidence before interpretation and could hide a crack that extends deeper.

steps.prompt: After solvent cleaning, the mark remains: a tight dark line that cuts across the wire-brush pattern instead of following it. How do you classify the indication?
options: False — probably glare from the brushed surface
Nonrelevant — it is just weld ripple geometry
Relevant — it persists after cleaning and behaves like a material discontinuity
steps.feedback: An indication that survives cleaning and crosses the surface texture is coming from the material, not from lighting or geometry — that is a relevant indication, and it now moves to evaluation against the criteria.

steps.prompt: Look at the acceptance criteria you were handed for this job. Which evaluation-criteria styles do they combine?
options: Purely verbal descriptions
Purely size-based limits
A mixture: verbal (no cracks), size-based (undercut and porosity limits), and comparison (workmanship sample board)
steps.feedback: Real codes mix styles, and this paragraph uses all three. Treat it as a checklist: every relevant indication gets tested against the no-crack clause, the numeric limits, and the texture grading — not just the clause that is easiest to check.

steps.prompt: While you have the gauges out, the same weld shows a groove melted into the base metal along the toe. The criteria cap undercut at 1 mm deep. How do you evaluate it?
options: Drag a fingernail across it — if it catches, reject
Measure the depth with an undercut gauge such as a V-WAC that resolves finer than 1 mm, and compare the reading to the limit
Compare it visually to the workmanship sample board and assign a grade
steps.feedback: A numeric clause demands measurement with a tool that resolves finer than the limit. The fingernail drag is a fine detection aid and the sample board serves the texture clause, but neither produces the number the 1 mm criterion requires.

steps.prompt: Under the 10x magnifier the original linear indication shows fine branching with no displaced metal. You judge it a crack, 4 mm long. Disposition?
options: Accept — 4 mm is below any reasonable size limit in the criteria
Reject and record it as a defect: the verbal criterion allows no cracks of any size
Reclassify it as nonrelevant — it might be a mill seam rather than a service crack
steps.feedback: Verbal attribute criteria are binary. No cracks means no cracks — size limits in the same paragraph apply to undercut and porosity, not to cracks. The discontinuity is rejectable, so it is now properly called a defect, recorded, and reported for disposition.

checkQuestions.question: Which sequence states the general evaluation scheme in the correct order?
options: Indication → evaluation → interpretation → repair
Indication → interpretation → evaluation against acceptance criteria → disposition
Interpretation → indication → defect → evaluation
Evaluation → indication → disposition → interpretation
checkQuestions.explanation: You observe an indication, interpret it as false, nonrelevant, or relevant, evaluate relevant indications against the acceptance criteria, and only then disposition them.

checkQuestions.question: A bright line on a weld turns out to be glare from the wire-brush polish pattern, not any condition of the metal. How is the indication classified?
options: Relevant indication
Defect
False indication
Nonrelevant indication
checkQuestions.explanation: An indication produced by something other than an actual condition of the part — glare, smears, sheen — is false. Nonrelevant would describe a real condition, like geometry, that the criteria do not address.

checkQuestions.question: What must be recorded when grading a casting surface against a set of comparison plates?
options: Only the final accept or reject result
The grade assigned, the identity or serial of the comparator set, and the criteria clause invoking it
The chemistry of the casting alloy
A photograph in place of the grade — grading is otherwise informal
checkQuestions.explanation: Comparison evaluation is only repeatable if the report names the exact reference standard used, the grade assigned, and the criteria clause that called for it.

checkQuestions.question: An acceptance criterion limits undercut depth to one thirty-second of an inch. Which evaluation approach does this criterion force?
options: Visual-tactile recognition alone
Grading against a photographic catalog
Measurement with a tool that resolves finer than the limit, such as a V-WAC or bridge-cam gauge
Estimating depth by eye from the normal viewing distance
checkQuestions.explanation: A numeric limit demands a measured value from a tool with finer resolution than the limit itself; recognition and grading cannot produce the required number.

checkQuestions.question: Why does a relevant indication that passes evaluation NOT become a defect?
options: Because defect means rejectable — an acceptable discontinuity is recorded and accepted
Because passing evaluation proves no discontinuity exists
Because only cracks can ever be called defects
Because the word defect applies only after a repair attempt fails
checkQuestions.explanation: Defect is reserved for discontinuities that violate the acceptance criteria. A real discontinuity can remain in the part, documented and accepted, without ever being a defect.

references: CP-105-2024 VT Level II Topical Outline §8.1–8.3
ASTM E1316, Standard Terminology for Nondestructive Examinations (indication, interpretation, evaluation, defect)
Structural and pressure-code acceptance criteria (generic examples, e.g., AWS D1.1-style undercut limits)
